hard.next_step: 5/19MF - met with Anand, Braden. Focus of conversation and what we presented was intake, supplier onboarding, automated risk assessments as well as an overview of the broader S2P suite so he can make a case for change. As a next step we'll be sending him questions we need answers to build out ROI for his board meeeting scheduled to take place 6/23 as well as some sample customer names who would have equally stringent security requirements, aka Federal customers, BFSI, healthcare customers etc. 5/7MF - spoke with Anand. Corus is undergoing restructuring which will be completed this month. They want to see the platform in order to present at an upcoming board meeting a case for budget to be allocated starting in new FY which start Sept 1. Esker currently used for ERP. A vision was laid out for an end-to-end procure-to-pay solution featuring conversational intake, automated risk assessments, supplier onboarding. Next step is a DC with Braden scheduled for May 19th. Other key stakeholders will be introduced if the project gets board approval to build a business case and formalize requirements.
hard.ais_why: No recorded calls yet, but the SF next step captures Mike's vision for Corus as an end-to-end procure-to-pay solution featuring conversational intake, automated risk assessments, supplier onboarding. Anand is taking a case to a board meeting to secure FY budget starting 1 Sept; appetite is there but no transcripted customer voice yet, so the AI cues come from rep-side framing, keeping confidence Low.

north_star_verdict.headline: Waiting on the 23 Jun board budget vote to advance; that gate passed 13 days ago with no confirmed outcome, and there has been no buyer reply since the 19 May demo (48 days). Single-threaded through one mid-level champion in a mid-restructuring org.
north_star_verdict.math: 178 days to close (31 Dec 2026); opp is 60 days old and has spent all 60 in Qualified with zero stage movement (field history). Last buyer-facing interaction was the 19 May overview demo, 48 days ago (short of the 60-day cold threshold, so Slowing not Off Track). The forcing gate was the 23 Jun board budget meeting, now 13 days past with no confirmation the promised ROI inputs or references were delivered or that budget was approved. To hold a 31 Dec close the deal must still traverse Formal Evaluation, Shortlisted, Vendor Selected, Negotiation and Contract, roughly 30-35 days per stage, while it is stalled at the first gate.
evidence: Last buyer engagement: 19 May 2026 overview demo with Anand and Braden, 48 days ago (SF Next Step 19 May)
Forcing gate: 23 Jun board budget meeting, 13 days past, no confirmed outcome (SF Next Step 19 May)
Promised deliverables (ROI-input questions + security reference names) committed 19 May, no confirmation they were sent (SF Next Step 19 May)
Single-threaded: zero OpportunityContactRole records; only Anand and Braden named, both in rep notes (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May)
No economic buyer, decision maker, or board sponsor identified (SF, no contact roles)
Stage velocity: 60 days in Qualified with no progression against 178 days to close over 5+ remaining stages (SF field history)

metrics.narrative: The business problem is articulated qualitatively but never quantified. On 7 May Anand said Corus is building a case for new-fiscal-year budget (starting 1 Sept 2026) for a procure-to-pay transformation, and on 19 May the demo covered conversational intake, supplier onboarding and automated risk assessments so Anand could make a case for change (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May). No cost-savings target, headcount reduction, cycle-time or error-rate figure was captured, and there is no buyer call to anchor a quantified impact. Zycus committed 19 May to send ROI-input questions to help Anand build the number for the board, but there is no confirmation those were delivered. Quantifying the value case with Anand is the ranked move; without it the board vote likely lacked financial grounding.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is named or mapped. The evidence points to the Corus board as the budget authority: Anand said Corus wants to present a case for budget to be allocated starting the new fiscal year (1 Sept) at an upcoming board meeting held 23 Jun (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May). Anand is a mid-level sponsor carrying the case upward, not the person who controls the commercial or pricing decision, and there is no OpportunityContactRole, no finance stakeholder, and no board member on record. The move is to have Anand name and introduce the finance owner / board sponsor who controls the FY budget, then confirm the 23 Jun outcome; until then budget authority sits behind a board vote we cannot see into.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are partially visible from the 19 May demo and the requested proof points. The buyer's interest centres on conversational/agentic intake, supplier onboarding and automated risk assessments across a broad S2P suite (SF Next Step 19 May), and Anand asked for sample customers with equally stringent security requirements, naming Federal, BFSI and healthcare as the comparators (SF Next Step 19 May) which signals security and compliance are hard criteria. These are inferred from rep notes rather than confirmed on a buyer call, and no formal requirements document or scored evaluation exists yet. Formalizing requirements was explicitly deferred until after board approval (SF Next Step 7 May).

decision_process.narrative: The process the buyer is running is a two-step internal one: build a platform view and business case, then present to the board on 23 Jun for new-FY budget approval, after which other key stakeholders would be introduced to formalize requirements (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May). So budget approval is the first real gate and it gates everything downstream, including access to the wider committee. That gate passed 13 days ago with no confirmed outcome. There is no visibility into the board membership, the approval chain, or timelines beyond the 1 Sept fiscal-year start; reconstructing the post-board process with Anand is the next step.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics are in scope yet; the deal sits in Qualified and paper process is genuinely unstarted. Anand's request for security references (Federal, BFSI, healthcare comparators) foreshadows an InfoSec/security-review gate later (SF Next Step 19 May), but no MSA, SOW, order form, legal, procurement channel or contract-expiration forcing function has surfaced. Visibility here will only come once budget is approved and requirements are formalized; there is nothing to redline while the deal is pre-budget.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and operational, though owned by a single mid-level sponsor rather than an executive. Corus completed a restructuring in May 2026 and is modernizing procure-to-pay as part of new-FY budget planning; Esker is currently used for ERP and the vision is an end-to-end P2P platform with conversational intake, automated risk assessments and supplier onboarding (SF Next Step 7 May). The inferred pain is manual intake, supplier-onboarding friction and a lack of automated risk/compliance workflows, with Anand as the pain owner. The restructuring is a genuine forcing function, but the pain is not yet quantified and not yet sponsored by a senior executive, which weakens its urgency at the board.

champion.narrative: Anand is the only identified champion and his strength is weak. He initiated the engagement on 7 May, brought Braden to the 19 May demo, and is building the business case for the 23 Jun board meeting (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May), which is genuine advocacy. But he has not introduced any economic buyer or board member, has provided no access to the decision layer, and has gone quiet for 48 days with no confirmation the board approved budget or that the promised ROI/reference materials were used. A strong champion would have shared the board outcome within days and escalated internally. Anand's silence signals either a board deferral he is not pushing back on, or lost urgency post-restructuring; the deal is single-threaded with no backup champion and no contact roles.

competition.narrative: The primary contest is inertia, not a named rival. Esker is deployed for ERP at Corus (SF Next Step 7 May), and Zycus is positioning to expand beyond it with AI-native P2P (Merlin conversational intake, iRisk automated risk, full S2P suite). No formal RFP, bake-off or second vendor has been named, so the real competitor is do-nothing: Corus can keep running Esker and manual processes and simply defer the investment if the board withholds new-FY budget. The wedge is agentic intake and automated compliance versus Esker's narrower footprint, but the buyer has not stated Esker is failing, so the advantage is unconfirmed. Winning means reframing the ROI around the AI/automation gap and giving Anand a quantified case the board cannot defer.

deal_movement.summary: The deal has been quiet since the 19 May overview demo, 48 days ago. The only subsequent tracked event is the rep-noted 23 Jun board budget meeting, now 13 days past with no confirmed outcome and no buyer reply. Stage has not moved off Qualified in 60 days; a division reclassification on 26 May is an internal change, not buyer momentum.

competitive_position.summary: The strongest current threat is inertia / no-decision, not a vendor: with new-FY budget unapproved, Corus can keep running Esker for ERP and defer the P2P investment (SF Next Step 7 May). Esker is the incumbent whose narrower footprint Zycus aims to displace, but no second vendor, RFP or bake-off has been named in any source. We win by converting Anand's board case into a quantified, AI-led ROI the board cannot postpone.

best_practice_check.summary: Three levers raise win probability now: break the single thread, map the budget authority behind the board vote, and quantify the ROI so the board case survives scrutiny.
flags: Break the single thread: only Anand is engaged with zero contact roles; secure a second contact (Braden or the finance/budget owner) before the board decision ages further.
Map the budget authority: the economic buyer is the board and is unmapped; have Anand name and introduce the finance owner who controls the new-FY budget.
Quantify the business case: no ROI numbers exist; deliver the promised ROI-input questions and build the savings/automation case Corus needs to justify budget.

items.name: Anand

items.name: Braden
items.risk: Attended the 19 May demo; role unclear and no subsequent engagement. Potential second thread if role and authority are confirmed.

champion_strength.summary: Anand is the sole champion and weak. He initiated the deal, ran the 19 May demo and is building the board case, but he has introduced no economic buyer, given no access to power, and gone silent for 48 days including 13 days past the 23 Jun board gate with no outcome shared. Single-threaded, no backup, no contact roles; at real risk of disengaging if the board deferred or his sponsorship faded post-restructuring.
champion_strength.source: SF Next Step 7 & 19 May 2026; Avoma 0 buyer calls; SF OpportunityContactRole (empty)

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The agentic story (Merlin conversational intake, iRisk automated risk, Agentic AI) is central to the vision Mike framed and is a genuine wedge over Esker, but it has landed only through rep-side notes, never validated by the buyer on a call. It is under-positioned until a buyer voice confirms the AI value and it anchors a quantified ROI.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 5.0 / Moderate. The vision names conversational/agentic intake and automated risk assessments, so appetite exists, but it is rep-framed with no transcripted customer voice, so the signal is unvalidated rather than proven.

The last real interaction was the 19 May overview demo with Anand and Braden; nothing has come back in the 48 days since, including from the 23 Jun board vote.

There is no pricing, ROI, or budget confirmation; the $150k is a placeholder pending a board vote whose outcome is unknown.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Corus is an early, single-threaded Qualified deal riding on one mid-level champion (Anand) building an internal budget case; the 23 Jun board vote that gates all funding passed 13 days ago with no confirmed outcome, and there has been no buyer reply since the 19 May overview demo.

win_position.text: Win position is low: this is a first-gate Qualified deal with one weak mid-level champion, no economic buyer engaged, and no buyer voice on any recorded call, only rep-written notes (SF Next Step 7 & 19 May).
The budget itself is unapproved and hinges on the 23 Jun board vote, whose outcome Anand has not shared 13 days on (SF Next Step 19 May).
The deal is single-threaded through Anand with no finance or board sponsor mapped, so if he loses urgency after the May restructuring there is no backup (SF, zero contact roles).

deal_momentum.text: Momentum has stalled: no buyer-facing interaction since the 19 May overview demo, 48 days ago, and 60 days in Qualified with no stage movement (SF field history).
The forcing event, the 23 Jun board budget meeting, came and went with no confirmation the promised ROI inputs or references were even delivered (SF Next Step 19 May).

customer_commitment.text: Commitment is early and internal: Anand wants a platform view to build a board case for new-FY budget starting 1 Sept, but no commercial step, pricing ask, or Zycus-specific preference has been voiced (SF Next Step 7 May).
No economic buyer or board sponsor has been introduced, and other stakeholders were explicitly deferred until after board approval (SF Next Step 7 May).

deal_risk.text: The primary risk is inertia, not a rival: Corus keeps running Esker for ERP and may simply defer the P2P investment if the board withholds new-FY budget (SF Next Step 7 May).
A mid-restructuring org (completed May 2026) makes sponsorship and budget fragile, and a weak single champion who has gone quiet compounds no-decision risk (SF Next Step 7 May).
